perturbation.original_text: Volunteer , Trainer or Evaluator further agrees that upon t ermination of se rvice with Companion Paws and The LifeLine Canada Foundation , Volunteer , Trainer or Evaluator shall return within ten (10) business days all documents, files, e lectronic or otherwise, and property comprising Confidential Information of Companion Paws and The LifeLine Canada Foundation and all copies, electronic or otherwise, thereof relating in any way to Companion Paws and The LifeLine Canada Foundation’s busine ss, or in any way obtained during the course of service. Volunteer or Evaluator further agrees that no copies, electronic or otherwise, notes or abstracts of the foregoing shall be retained and that all information will be returned not destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: Volunteer , Trainer or Evaluator further agrees that upon t ermination of se rvice with Companion Paws and The LifeLine Canada Foundation , Volunteer , Trainer or Evaluator shall return all documents, files, e lectronic or otherwise, and property comprising Confidential Information of Companion Paws and The LifeLine Canada Foundation and all copies, electronic or otherwise, thereof relating in any way to Companion Paws and The LifeLine Canada Foundation’s busine ss, or in any way obtained during the course of service.
perturbation.explanation: The clause requiring the volunteer to return all copies and certify destruction of information has been removed. This potentially contradicts privacy laws if the volunteer retains personal information of Canadian citizens obtained during their service, as it removes the explicit obligation to destroy this data.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA)
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA requires organizations to protect personal information and specifies that organizations must destroy, erase, or render anonymous personal information that is no longer required to fulfill the purpose for which it was collected. By removing the requirement for the volunteer to destroy copies of confidential information, the modified text potentially contradicts PIPEDA if that information contains personal data of Canadian citizens.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the explicit requirement for the volunteer to destroy copies of confidential information. The Law Explanation states that PIPEDA requires organizations to destroy or render anonymous personal information that is no longer required. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that this omission potentially contradicts privacy laws if the volunteer retains personal information of Canadian citizens, as it removes the obligation to destroy this data. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions PIPEDA's requirements and refers to compliance help and guidance for businesses to understand and comply with the law. This supports the idea that organizations, including those using volunteers, must comply with PIPEDA, and the omission of the data destruction requirement creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Volunteer , Trainer or Evaluator agrees to maintain the confidentiality of all Confidential Information and not misuse, misappropriate, or disclose in writing , orally or by electronic means, any Confidential Information, directly or indirectly, to any other person or use them in any way, either during the term of this Agreement or at any other time thereafter, except as is required in th e course of service to C ompanion Paws and The LifeLine Canada Foundation .
perturbation.changed_text: Volunteer , Trainer or Evaluator agrees to maintain the confidentiality of all Confidential Information and not misuse, misappropriate, or disclose in writing , orally or by electronic means, any Confidential Information, directly or indirectly, to any other person or use them in any way during the term of this Agreement, except as is required in th e course of service to C ompanion Paws and The LifeLine Canada Foundation .
perturbation.explanation: The original text contains an agreement to maintain confidentiality 'at any other time thereafter', this has been removed. This introduces a legal contradiction as it removes the protection of confidential information after the agreement terminates, which violates standard business practice and could conflict with legal protections for trade secrets and confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Canadian law regarding trade secrets and confidential information (common law principles and potential statutory provisions)
perturbation.law_citation: Common Law Principles of Confidentiality and Trade Secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: Canadian common law recognizes the protection of confidential information and trade secrets. By limiting the confidentiality obligation to only the term of the agreement, the modified text fails to protect Companion Paws' confidential information after the agreement ends, potentially enabling volunteers to misuse or disclose sensitive data without legal recourse after the agreement has ended.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: No Fault Divorce In England & Wales: A Withersworldwide Tour - Divorce - Worldwide ARTICLE 4 April 2022 No Fault Divorce In England & Wales: A Withersworldwide Tour WL Withers LLP More Contributor Trusted advisors to successful people and businesses across the globe with complex legal needs Explore Firm Details With 'no fault divorce' being introduced in England in a couple of weeks' time, I thought it was worth a quick look at the situation in some other parts of the world where Withers offers family law advice to clients... Worldwide Family and Matrimonial Suzanne Todd Your Author LinkedIn Connections To print this article, all you need is to be registered or login on Mondaq.com. With 'no fault divorce' being introduced in England in a couple of weeks' time, I thought it was worth a quick look at the situation in some other parts of the world where Withers offers family law advice to clients – Hong Kong, Singapore and the US. Practitioners in England (including me) have long campaigned for the introduction of no fault divorce. As the law stands now (before the changes due to be brought in on 6 April 2022), the spouse who files for divorce must 'prove' that the marriage has broken down to the court. They typically have to do this either by living apart from their spouse for a minimum period of two years and (in addition) obtaining their spouse's agreement to the divorce; by living apart from their spouse for five years (with no additional requirement to obtain their spouse's agreement); or by blaming their spouse for the breakdown of the marriage due to their adultery or unreasonable behaviour. This can often set a bad tone to proceedings at the outset, which may not have been overcome by the time the couple is trying to make arrangements for any children or trying to finalise a financial deal. Under the new law, the court will accept the couple's word for it that their marriage has broken down without further proof and, for the first time, they can also apply for a divorce jointly instead of one spouse needing to divorce the other. This is a positive step for a lot of couples and it means that proceedings can start off on the right foot. However, there is no one-size-fits-all system to suit everybody as both people and relationships are diverse. A lot of people believe that things happen for a reason (not just that they happen), and they wonder whether the new law will fail to meet some people's cathartic need to explain why their marriage broke down in the way that the current system allows them to. The good news there, however, is that there will remain support for couples to help those needs to be met in the form of divorce therapy and/or the many new and alternative forms of dispute resolution. As we will see, it is not only the law of England and Wales which is changing. In Singapore, an additional fact for no fault divorce was introduced early this year which allows parties to file for divorce by mutual agreement. So let's turn to have a look at other countries' divorce laws. Hong Kong and Singapore Hong Kong and Singapore's legal systems were both developed under British governance and so many of their laws look familiar to the English. The grounds on which a divorce can be obtained in either Hong Kong or Singapore bear a lot of resemblance to the current law in England. An application (which can be joint) can be made in Hong Kong based on the parties having been separated for as little as a year (which is less than the current law in England). Alternatively, one can proceed with a divorce using one of the following four grounds to prove that the marriage has irretrievably broken down: Adultery Unreasonable behaviour Two years separation (and the other party does not additionally need to 'consent' to a divorce on the basis of this period of separation as s/he would need to do under the current law in England) Desertion for one year In Singapore, meanwhile, one of the following five facts can be used to prove an irretr
perturbation.justification: The original text ensures confidentiality extends 'at any other time thereafter,' but the changed text limits this obligation to 'during the term of this Agreement.' The Perturbation Explanation indicates this weakens protection of confidential information after the agreement, potentially enabling misuse without legal recourse, as noted in the Law Explanation. While the scraped snippets do not directly address confidentiality, the removal of the 'at any other time thereafter' phrase directly contradicts the intent to protect confidential information beyond the agreement's duration, a standard business practice. Therefore, the modification creates a legal loophole, leading to a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Volunteer, Trainer or Evaluator shall act fairly and be impartial and unbiased in their service to the Companion Paws and The LifeLine Canada Foundation and to the population of Certificants and candidates.
perturbation.changed_text: Volunteer, Trainer or Evaluator shall act fairly in their service to the Companion Paws and The LifeLine Canada Foundation and to the population of Certificants and candidates.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'impartial and unbiased' weakens the commitment to fairness. This creates a legal contradiction if the volunteer acts in a way that shows preference or prejudice towards particular candidates, which could violate anti-discrimination laws or principles of fair dealing, particularly if the program is a federally funded program.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 2 of the Canadian Human Rights Act prohibits discrimination based on various grounds. By removing the explicit requirement for impartiality and lack of bias, the modified text creates a risk that volunteers could discriminate against candidates or certificants, potentially violating the Act, if the services were considered publicly available or subsidized by the government.
perturbation.location: Volunteer , Trainer or Evaluator Code of Conduct section
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Homepage | Canadian Human Rights Commission Canadian Human Rights Commission All topics For individuals Discrimination and harassment, accessibility, duty to accommodate, housing, human rights, pay equity For organizations Employer responsibilities, deadlines, plans, respond to a complaint Find help Find help on discrimination and harassment, complaint rules, accessibility, housing, pay equity matters, as well as information on emergency support services and provincial and territorial human rights agencies Resources Newsroom, publications, forms, policies, templates, tools and portals Our work Advocating for human rights, screening complaints, litigating cases, monitoring and promoting compliance Recent highlights Housing rights for people with disabilities Read about our project to monitor whether Canada is making progress on its human rights obligations Workplace accommodation Read the Commission's guide for federally regulated employers on accommodating the needs of employees Accessibility progress report Read about our latest progress in creating a more barrier-free Commission Report a problem on this page
perturbation.justification: The original text requires volunteers to act 'fairly and be impartial and unbiased,' while the modified text omits 'impartial and unbiased.' The Law Explanation states that removing the requirement for impartiality creates a risk of discrimination, potentially violating the Canadian Human Rights Act. Snippet 2 mentions the Canadian Human Rights Commission and topics like discrimination and harassment. The Perturbation Explanation states that removing these terms could violate anti-discrimination laws. This suggests a clear contradiction, as the modified text weakens a commitment that aligns with human rights law, creating a risk of discriminatory actions not present in the original agreement. The removal thus contradicts the principles outlined in the Canadian Human Rights Act, specifically regarding discrimination.

perturbation.original_text: Volunteer, Trainer or Evaluator acknowledges that a violation of the terms of this Agreement ma y cause damage and harm to Companion P aws and The LifeLine Canada Foundation and that any such damage or harm will be difficult if not impos sible to calculate in monetary terms and will be irre parable to Companion Paws and The LifeLine Canada Foundation . Volunteer , Trainer and Evaluator agrees that, upon n otice from Companion Paws and The LifeLine Canada Foundation declaring a breach of this A greement, Volunteer , Trainer or Evaluator shall immediately cease all further activities which are, or are claimed by Companion Paws and The LifeLine Canada Foundation to be, a breach of this Agreement. Companion Paws and The LifeLine Canada Foundation may also avail itself of any other remedies available by law.
perturbation.changed_text: Volunteer, Trainer or Evaluator acknowledges that a violation of the terms of this Agreement ma y cause damage and harm to Companion P aws and The LifeLine Canada Foundation. Volunteer , Trainer and Evaluator agrees that, upon n otice from Companion Paws and The LifeLine Canada Foundation declaring a breach of this A greement, Volunteer , Trainer or Evaluator shall immediately cease all further activities which are, or are claimed by Companion Paws and The LifeLine Canada Foundation to be, a breach of this Agreement. Companion Paws and The LifeLine Canada Foundation may also avail itself of any other remedies available by law.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the statement that damages will be difficult to calculate and irreparable, the document suggests monetary compensation could be sufficient. This could limit the organisation's ability to seek injunctive relief, contradicting principles of Canadian law regarding equitable remedies for breach of contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Canadian Contract Law - Equitable Remedies (Injunctive Relief)
perturbation.law_citation: Principles of Equitable Remedies in Canadian Contract Law
perturbation.law_explanation: Canadian contract law recognizes equitable remedies such as injunctive relief, which may be available when monetary damages are insufficient. By removing the acknowledgement that damages would be difficult to calculate and would be irreparable, the agreement implies that monetary damages are adequate, potentially hindering Companion Paws' ability to seek an injunction to prevent further harm from a breach of contract. This undermines the common law principle that equitable remedies are available when legal remedies (monetary damages) are inadequate.
perturbation.justification: The original text acknowledges that damages would be difficult to calculate and would be irreparable. The changed text removes this acknowledgement. According to the Perturbation and Law Explanations, this suggests that monetary damages are adequate, potentially hindering the organization's ability to seek equitable remedies like an injunction. Even with the absence of the law snippets, the logic presented in the explanations strongly indicates a legally relevant contradiction.
